transcript.clean_text: - I'm here at the Vuelta Espana, and I'm having a good old snoop around the pro's bikes. Part of being a pro cyclist has to be going as fast as possible through the wind, well that's what winner does isn't it? To get to the finish line first. Now some of the fancy bits of tech to do this, can cost a fair bit of money, another ways can come for free, and whilst I'm at it, I'm going to look for some lightweight bits of kit too. Now if you want the ultimate in aerodynamics, and clean look on your bike, forget about heat shrinking cables together, or spiral wrapping them together, get this, a handlebar instead, which allows you to run all of those cables, or hoses in this case, all internally, perfectly, so that the only bit of cable or hose you have showing, for these disc brake bikes, are actually just popping out of the stays, or also down there on the rear of the chain stay, where that little DI2 cable comes out. That is as aero as you can get on the cockpit, if you ask me. Now if you're able to keep a bike nice and stable, in some good old fashioned crosswinds, how about using one of these, a 90-millimeter deep wheel. This is as fast as you can get almost, without going silly, so in other words, using a tri spoke, or a five spoke, that kind of thing. Nice. Wow, look at this, on the Colnago Concept of UAE Team Emirates, this headset, top cap, look at it, it's shaped aerodynamically, to blend in with the frame. I have seen these available as aftermarket products, and in fact, a friend of mine years ago, actually made one, out of a bit of broken old carbon fiber, so if you're handy with the carbon, maybe you can make one too. How about this then, a saddle from Prologo. It's got kind of like an aerodynamic fairing, on the underside, and that's UCI legal. It's quite surprising that, I have never seen one of these, and then mounted onto the down tube here, we've actually got a bottle cage from Elite, which will allow you to fit in one of those oversized type water bottles, which gets you basically through the wind a little bit easier. This stem top cap here from Carbon-Ti has a carbon fiber main body, and then the bolt, well that's titanium, so you can see where the name comes from: Carbon, Ti. Anyway, that's going to save the rider probably, say 10 grams compared to a quite heavy alloy one. One of my favorite aerodynamic bits and pieces to help you go faster has to be these bar end plugs from Envy. So rather than the traditional ones which just pop in the end, these are actually teardrop shaped to help you go through the air a little bit faster; I do like those. ♪ Yeah ♪ Looking to save a handful of grams, well maybe the KMC X11 SL chain is for you. 243 grams, that one weighs in at, and that's because of the slotted pins, rivets, and also the side plates too. Compare that to Shimano Dura-Ace, they weigh in at 247 grams, so not a huge saving but well, at this level, every little bit counts doesn't it? Well how about this then: an aerodynamic chain ring. Rather than going out on the spider and having more areas for the actual wind to catch in, causing turbulence, and therefore drag, this is actually going to cut through the air slightly easier. Now I don't know about the science myself; I've not measured or done any tests, by they are becoming increasingly popular with riders. So those riders looking to go a little bit faster in the time trials, well many of the teams here are actually using these, the Continental Podium TT Pro Limited tubular tire. Fitted with a latex inner tube, so that's less rolling resistance when you're riding along, pretty important, obviously. And then the actual tread pattern is very different to the competition tubular tire that the riders use during the road stages. So this one as you can see has a kind of file pattern tread all over it and also the tread itself is a bit lighter weight than that road tire. Generally you are going to be saving probably between 50 and 60 grams per tire. Another area of the bike where you can lose quite a considerable amount of weight once it all adds up is the pedals; so these Look Keo Carbon Blades, normally they would weigh 110 grams a pedal, if you had the chromoly axle version. However, these ones have got a titanium spindle so it takes the weight down to 95 grams a pedal, so that's 30 grams over the pair, which, if you try to lose that weight elsewhere on a bike, could cost you quite a bit of money. And marginal gains, they are taken to the nth degree in professional cycling, but this stem, at first glance it looks pretty basic. However, it does in fact feature a camtel design. So once you get up to speed and you're blasting through the wind, that air is going to have a smoother runoff from the rear of the component compared to a standard one. Then at the front the stem, well, the actually faceplate doesn't have any visible bolts. Instead they're done from the reverse of the stem in order that you have a nice smooth surface here for the wind just to flow over. What about then if you really want to go about cheating the wind as best as you can? Well, do what the pros do: as you can see here, on the downtube, it's actually the cable routing, they blank up the holes using rubber bungs and such like. Often you see bits of tape too, but that ultimately is going to stop wind from entering in there, and kind of causing you a drag effect, because the wind is going to be, okay it's not going to be a lot, but it is going to be going inside there and slightly slowing you down. The time trial bike; they look funny, they've got funny angles, funny shapes, funny looks, let's face it, but there's something about them that always draws me in, despite the fact that when I was racing it was my least favorite discipline. Let's look though at what the pro mechanics do to actually try and get a little bit of extra speed out of them. First up, on the handlebars here, on the base bar, you can see a couple of strips of insulation tape. What are they there for? No, they're not covering up scratches or anything like that, they're actually covering up mounting holes of alternative positions for the tri bars to be fitted to. So that's actually going to smooth out the airflow. Then you've got other things just tucked away, like underneath the saddle here we've got the DI2 junction box, and that cable is then neatly tucked away down internally instead of having it out here in the wind. Now an area where bike manufacturers pay particular attention to, is this, the front, because obviously that's where the wind or air is hitting first. So in order to really smooth out this airflow, time trial bikes are commonly now coming with this kind of fairing-style system which covers up both the head tube to allow wind to flow around it a lot smoother, and also the brake calipers too, because it's quite a lot of intricate bits there and a lot of turbulence can be created which in turn gives you drag. Then another area of the bike which helps smooth out the airflow is here at the rear of the bike. So, if you do want to adjust the rear brake, you are in fact going to have to remove these few grub screws just to actually get in there and work on it. I'm sure the mechanics though, they absolutely love getting in there, to the nitty gritty of the internals of the braking system. These are one of the best ways riders can cut through the wind as fast as possible whilst riding in time trials. So, the spokes on them, they're shaped in such a way that the air exits that spoke, it doesn't create as much turbulence as it could do. And according to Xavier Disley, who's our aerodynamics expert that we often consult with here at GCN, he claims that these ones from PRO are one of the fastest wheels out there on the pro peloton. So there we are, just some of the bits of tech being used by the pros here at the Vuelta. Let me know your favorite bit of kit, though, down there in the comments section. Don't forget as well to give this video a big like, share, thumbs up, and let me know which of those bits of kit is your favorite, down there in the comments section. Remember as well, like and share this video with your friends, tell them all about it. Don't forget to check out The GCN Shop at shop.globalcyclingnetwork.com. You can spend your pocket money on a whole big variety of goodies out there. Also, Lloydie, every single day he is reviewing the stage of the Vuelta, so head on over to our Facebook page to see that. There is a link in the description beneath. I mean come on, let's go and keep Lloydie company over there; he knows his stuff doesn't he? Also, for another great video, click just down here.
